replies.author: @AlMcpherson79
replies.text:
@kdawg3484 I agree that it started then, but I think it really took off when Disney bought Marvel, because there was a strong sense of this: "Disney" produces movies that are mainly for girls (The Disney Princesses thing), but when they make stuff for boys, it tends to be under one of the other studios that they own. I never thought of Tron as a Disney product, but a Buena Vista. Which,... is owned by Disney. which I just learned because I just looked on wikipedia and just now learned that Honey I Shrunk the Kids was Disney, for some reason I thought it was nickelodeon or something like that. The Parent Trap. Father of the Bride. Sister Act. Yes, family movies, but not girl-focused in its marketing and merch etc. Yet back in 2010, when we thought of Disney, we thought of The Little Ginger. The Lion Doesnt Actually Look Weird Talking In This Form Of Animation. It Should Have Been Named Robin Williams As The Genie. Her Skin Is White and the Seven cannotfindpoliticallycorrectwordGeeThanksTyrianLannisters.
Comics were seen as something rarely consumed by girls, so why would movies based on them be watched by girls, etc. And Disney bought, and so you have on one side, Disney itself is a "Girl Brand" and now owns studios with their own marquees brand like Marvel and soon Lucasfilm, so they can also "get at the boys".


And it's not just disney. Everyone, by trying to "SuBvErT eXpEcTaTiOns" and making products whose target audience now EXCLUDES the very people it was actually consumed by in previous iterations (and I speak broadly of genres not just franchises) they've ruined it for everybody because here's the thing about say, the Star Trek Movies: There was always, and I mean ALWAYS a push for 'it needs to be watchable by people who HAVENT enjoyed the Original Series/The Next Generation". Guess what folks? I can't remember which of the two, it was, but it was one of these: The Wrath of Khan / The Undiscovered Country was my intro to Kirk! A random episode of TNG on BBC 2 when the BBC actually decided to go back to running them because snooker/tennis/football/cricket wasn't on, (only for a week later the next episode wouldn't be on) was my intro, but it was First Contact when I actually got to seriously watch PIcard and Co. By that point, my family had cable TV and so I was able to watch Voyager. Voyager was, more seriously, what got me INTO Star Trek. Deep Space Nine thou being the one that resulted in binge-watching, when I eventually got all the shows on VHS/DVD/etc. Way of the Warrior on VHS ended up with lots of errors from so much rewatching ha!) 

A good point was made by someone, that ... ahem, let me try a ferengi voice.   *shocked voice* There were FEE MALES in your fandom all along! The only reason the first trek movie, so referred shortly to as TMP, got made was because of a massive write-in to the studios that was organized... BY FEMALE FANS. Shocker, I know, but basically Star Trek was never all that exclusionary to women. All those conventions that resulting in the wider world having a terrible view of the "average trekkie"? Its nowhere near 100% male. There are literally people, today, that exist solely because their mom and dad met because of Star Trek. Or should I say, Grandparents.

Like think about it. 1970. Convention. various couples form from fans of TOS. by 1988, the now-grown children of those various couples meet in various combos at more conventions for TNG. 2006. The now-grown children of THOSE couples, so grand-children of the TOS fan couples, meet in various combos at more conventions for Voyager/Enterprise. ... 2024. The great-grandchildren of the TOS fans aren't going to meet. why would they? Disco is terrible, strange new worlds is terrible, and lower decks is basically supposed to be star trek for babies. 

Back to Star Wars though. It was loved around the globe. Who watched Ewan McGregor's The Long Way Around because they loved him in Star Wars, and grinned when he found people in the middle of noweh-how the sodding hell did they even see star wars because they recognise him as obi-wan and are asking for autographs!

These "boy brands" were never really *boy* brands. They were never really solely for *Americans* either. The only real consideration there was that it was american dollars from american theatres or american channels that the studios cared about (that's why they always focus on the US Domestic box office / etc and dont even say "US Domestic-" just "Domestic". 

It's why some films were imported into China, though eventually China realized the studios cared about the china box office too and started asking for shit, and that's why we get the hamfisted elements in Iron Man 3's china release that really was edited out for everywhere else, but also like, Age of Ultron.

And Finn getting shrunk on posters. Can't forget that one.

You need a protagonist who has an identity. So you can view the female replacements charitably as attempts to not just photocopy to produce a new Indy and a new Luke. And we live in a era of film where JJ and Rian Johnson are prominent directors. Johnson is a hack. JJ is basically Michael Bay with one extra trick. Good directors want to do what interests them. Playing in someone else's sandbox means that the directors are merely adequate or are newbs. Spielberg isn't putting up with Kathy. Fincher and Nolan would have zero interest. Tarantino might say yes as long as he can bring back Samuel L Jackson to say "use the force, motherf....". The obvious explanation is that Kathy is a clown hiring people she can control. The more pessimistic answer is that we've had 20 years of mediocrity and star wars requires a director who can make the spiritual side of it look compelling. So Ron Howard and James Mangold are more tolerable directors. We don't have a young Spielberg readymade who could direct ANYTHING and do it well.

replies.author: @Ol_Strange
replies.text: If I may interject - we're not all circle jerking, coolaid drinking fuckwits. The main problem the broadcast and film industry has is attrition. There are too few old hands knocking about because they either just got too old, fed up and simply moved on (working on a sound stage or location is always a weird, intense, physically and mentally demanding situation that always drags on longer than it needs to), were promoted out of the shop floor or were moved on by increasingly restrictive and cloying HR policies. The 'old guard' as it were just aren't there anymore to balance the scales - there isn't that big, hench, angry looking rigger there to look at people like utter shit when they say or do something stupid to put the fear of god into them and instead what you have is a a weird, self-contained world that is obsessed with it's own navel. Some of us are still knocking about, but we're on the periphery because it's better money as a consultant and also because fuck get ins and get outs.

author: @odysseuslost
text:
1:55 This is the story that, in my mind, perfectly encapsulates KK.  She was not a creative, she was an administrator.  She didn't know what made for a good story, and what she thought would make for a good story was instantly and instinctually rejected by anyone who had any passing familiarity with storytelling.  But she also lacked any self-awareness about this.  She saw people in positions of authority and decided that she should have a seat at the table without any real understanding or appreciation of what it took to have that seat.  She was able to accumulate administrative power through loyalty and connections, but she didn't understand that enterprises of art require a spark of genius that not everyone (including herself) has.

It's kind of like the accountant who does well in their fantasy football team and thinks that it somehow would translate to be a general manager of a professional team in real life.

replies.author: @thegrimharvest
replies.text:
​@babayaga6376the analogy: they bought a fine dining Italian restaurant and slowly replaced the entire menu with imitation vegan gruel while still keeping the labels. What do you mean you dont like it?! It's still Luigi's famous ragu, it's just new and better than the old version. Eat up, you'll learn to like it, unless you're a heckin hater and food critic. 

They assumed that we would have no other choice but to like it and keep shelling out money. But even if we didn't, unthinkable as that was... they could always count on the new audience to make up for the losses.

At every stop along the way they refused to course correct and instead doubled down, because investment in the message was more important, long term, than the short term losses. If they could just convince enough people that Luigi's always served imitation vegan gruel, and that it was better than the original recipes, it wouldn't matter if they lost the entire original customer base, the new replacement one would never know or care about the difference. 

They would eat it and believe it was amazing, or they would eat it out of loyalty to identity politics and claim it was amazing anyway.

Their calculation isn't about losing the original audience, it's whether they can replace them completely.

Of course the problem with that replacement audience is that they're never satisfied and constantly eating each other over their identity politics.

replies.author: @mikeguinness5408
replies.text:
@olafgurke4699 It's a good solid movie that could have been better.  Loads of people either aren't aware or forget that Tony Gilroy was brought in to re-write the screenplay because it was a mess.  He could only do so much because at that point they couldn't start over with a new script and film a movie all over again.  Rogue One had a good premise about how the Rebellion got the plans to the Death Star.  Problem was we got all these brand new characters we didn't know anything about and the way the film was paced they didn't get to develop.  I think Andor does help and changes watching Rogue One now.  We have some more detail about some characters, have seen their character arcs, and not only do we now know them, but we are invested in them as well.  Yeah, mostly Cassian, but there's others that are now at least more interesting.  
There's that certain lot who don't care for Andor, either saying it's not Star Wars (whatever their idea of Star Wars is, maybe influenced by RedLetterMedia?) or that it was boring.  Well, I was a kid when A New Hope first hit theaters and I've been on board from the start.  I'm a nerdy fan of the OG, liked the Prequels (unlike the RLM crowd), read over 60 of the Expanded Universe books (enjoyed most of them, but eventually got burned out and fatigued on the overuse of the OG characters), like the animated shows The Clone Wars and Rebels, and liked the first 2 season of The Mandalorian.  So for all of that I also LOVED Andor.  Such a well done show on every level and I enjoyed seeing the shadowy underbelly of the Empire and the Rebellion.  Not everything in the Star Wars galaxy is spaceship battles, lightsabres, and Jedi.  Andor is Star Wars to me, just a different POV of it.  Feel sorry for those who can't enjoy different things.
